reasoning:
As of the knowledge cutoff date of November 9, 2025, the Federal Reserve has implemented only two interest rate cuts during 2025: a 25 basis point cut in September and another 25 basis point cut at the meeting ending in late October/early November. This brings the total easing for the year to 50 basis points (equivalent to 2 'cuts' of 25 bps). The target federal funds rate range currently stands at 3.75% to 4.00%, down from the 4.25% to 4.50% range at the end of 2024.

For this market to resolve 'Yes', the Fed would need to implement a total of at least 8 cuts (200 bps) by the conclusion of the December 2025 meeting. Since only 2 cuts have been made so far, an additional 6 cuts (150 bps) would be required at the final meeting of the year. Given that current economic data shows inflation remains slightly elevated (2.4%-2.9%) and unemployment is at 4.5%, there is no indication of an economic crisis that would trigger a massive 150 bps emergency-style cut. FOMC projections (dot plots) throughout late 2025 consistently signaled a year-end rate between 3.4% and 4.4%, which is far above the ~2.5% required for 8 cuts. Therefore, the probability of reaching the 8-cut threshold is extremely remote.
